questions.question:
A process executes the following segment of code:

for (i = 1; i <= n; i++)
    fork();

The number of new processes created is
A. n
B. n(n+1)/2
C. 2^n - 1
D. 3^n - 1
questions.to_find: Total number of new (child) processes created when fork() is called n times in a loop

understand.plain: A process runs a loop from i=1 to n, calling fork() each iteration. Each fork() duplicates the currently running process. So at each step, every existing process calls fork() again, doubling the total count. We need to find how many new processes are created in total (not counting the original).

keywords.explain: A system call that creates an exact copy of the calling process; both parent and child continue executing from the point after fork().
keywords.example: One process calls fork() -> now 2 processes exist.

keywords.explain: The hierarchical structure showing parent-child relationships among all processes spawned by fork calls.
keywords.example: After 2 forks: original spawns child1, then both original and child1 each spawn another child, giving 4 total processes.

understand.visual_alt: Process tree showing doubling at each fork iteration. After n iterations, 2^n total processes exist, so 2^n - 1 new processes are created (excluding the original).

given.aim: Find the total number of new processes created by n sequential fork() calls inside a loop

terms.meaning: Number of loop iterations, each calling fork() once
terms.example: n=3 means 3 fork() calls in the loop
terms.connects: Determines how many times the process count doubles

terms.meaning: After k fork() calls, there are 2^k total processes
terms.connects: After n iterations, 2^n total processes exist

terms.meaning: Total processes minus the original process
terms.example: If 2^n total exist, then 2^n - 1 are new

given.plan: Track how many processes exist after each fork(). Before the loop: 1 process. After iteration i: all existing processes call fork(), so the count doubles. After n iterations: 2^n processes total. New processes = 2^n - 1.

steps.title: Count processes at each iteration
steps.apply: Initially 1 process. At i=1: fork() is called by 1 process -> 2 processes. At i=2: both processes call fork() -> 4 processes. At i=3: all 4 call fork() -> 8 processes. In general, after k iterations: 2^k processes.
steps.note: Every existing process executes the loop and calls fork() independently, so the count doubles each iteration.

steps.title: Compute number of new processes
steps.apply: After all n iterations, there are 2^n total processes. The original process was there before the loop. So the number of NEW processes created = 2^n - 1.
steps.note: This is option C. The answer n(n+1)/2 (option B) is wrong; it would apply to a different counting scenario.

solution.result: 2^n - 1 new processes are created. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: fork() doubles the number of running processes each time it is called; after n calls in sequence by all descendants, 2^n total processes exist, giving 2^n - 1 new ones.

questions.question:
Which of the following is an example of spooled device?
A. A line printer used to print the output of a number of jobs
B. A terminal used to enter input data to a running program
C. A secondary storage device in a virtual memory system
D. A graphic display device
questions.answer: A. A line printer used to print the output of a number of jobs

understand.plain: Spooling (Simultaneous Peripheral Operations On-Line) is a technique where output data from multiple jobs is buffered on disk before being sent to a slow device. A line printer is the classic spooled device: multiple jobs submit output to a spool queue, and the printer processes them one at a time. The other options (terminal, secondary storage for virtual memory, graphics display) are not spooled devices.

keywords.explain: A method where I/O requests from multiple processes are queued (spooled) to disk and processed by the device sequentially.
keywords.example: Multiple programs print to a shared printer; their outputs are first written to a disk spool and printed one by one.

keywords.explain: A slow output device shared by many jobs, typically managed via a print spool queue.
keywords.example: In a batch OS, 10 jobs generate output; all outputs are spooled to disk and printed sequentially.

understand.visual_alt: Multiple jobs send output to a disk spool queue; the printer reads from the spool and prints jobs one at a time.

given.aim: Identify which device among the options uses spooling

terms.meaning: A device whose requests from multiple processes are buffered in a queue (spool) and served sequentially
terms.example: A shared printer collecting print jobs from many users

terms.meaning: A slow, shared output device that can only serve one job at a time
terms.example: Output of batch jobs queued to disk and printed sequentially
terms.connects: Classic spooled device in OS textbooks

terms.example: Page faults cause pages to be loaded from disk into RAM
terms.connects: Not a spooled device; eliminates option C

given.plan: Recall the definition of spooling: buffering I/O for slow shared devices. Match that definition to the options. A line printer shared among many jobs is the textbook example of a spooled device.

steps.title: Define spooling
steps.apply: Spooling = Simultaneous Peripheral Operations On-Line. It buffers the output of multiple jobs to disk and feeds it to a slow device (e.g., printer) one at a time. The key features are: shared slow device, multiple competing jobs, and a disk-based queue.
steps.note: Spooling increases CPU and device utilization by decoupling fast CPU output from slow device I/O.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply:
A. Line printer printing output of many jobs: YES — classic spool. Multiple jobs write to spool; printer reads from it.
B. Terminal for entering input: NO — interactive, direct I/O, not spooled.
C. Secondary storage for virtual memory: NO — used for paging, not spooling.
D. Graphic display device: NO — directly driven by one process at a time.
steps.note: Only A fits the definition of a spooled device.

solution.result: A line printer used to print the output of a number of jobs. Answer: A.

questions.formula_note: Spooling (Simultaneous Peripheral Operations On-Line): buffers output from multiple jobs on disk before sending to a slow shared device like a printer.

questions.question:
Which of the following is an example of a spooled device?
A. The terminal used to enter the input data for the C program being executed
B. An output device used to print the output of a number of jobs
C. The secondary memory device in a virtual storage system
D. The swapping area on a disk used by the swapper
questions.answer: B. An output device used to print the output of a number of jobs
questions.to_find: Which device is a spooled device

understand.plain: This is the same concept as the previous question (GATE 1996) but with slightly reworded options. Spooling is used for slow shared output devices (like printers) where multiple jobs queue their output to disk and the device processes them sequentially. A printer used by many jobs is the spooled device. Terminals (interactive), secondary memory for virtual storage (paging), and swap areas (swapping) are all different I/O mechanisms.

keywords.explain: Buffering multiple jobs' output to a spool (disk queue) before sending to a slow shared device.
keywords.example: 10 programs want to print; their outputs go into a spool on disk; printer picks them one by one.

keywords.meaning: The area on disk used to temporarily hold entire processes swapped out of RAM.
keywords.explain: Part of virtual memory management, not related to spooling.
keywords.example: When memory is full, a process is swapped to disk swap area.

understand.visual_alt: A printer shared among multiple jobs: each job writes output to a disk spool, and the printer reads from the spool in order.

given.aim: Identify the spooled device among the given options

terms.meaning: A slow shared device whose I/O is buffered via a spool queue on disk
Interactive input/output device, directly connected to a running process
terms.example: User types input for a C program via keyboard

terms.meaning: Disk space reserved for swapping out entire process address spaces
terms.example: Swapper moves a low-priority process from RAM to swap disk

given.plan: Apply spooling definition: slow shared output device, multiple producers, disk buffer. Only option B describes a printer serving multiple jobs, which is the canonical spooled device.

steps.title: Recall spooling definition
steps.apply: Spooling buffers data from multiple jobs on disk and feeds it to a slow device like a printer. The OS maintains a spool directory on disk where jobs write their output; a separate daemon reads from the spool and sends data to the physical device.
steps.note: Spooling decouples the fast CPU from the slow I/O device and allows multiple jobs to 'print' concurrently.

steps.title: Match to options
steps.apply:
A. Terminal for C program input: interactive, real-time I/O, not spooled.
B. Output device printing output of many jobs: YES — spool queue holds outputs of multiple jobs; device serves them one by one.
C. Secondary memory in virtual storage: used for paging (page-in/page-out), not spooling.
D. Swapping area: used for process swapping, not spooling.

solution.result: An output device used to print the output of a number of jobs. Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: Spooling: Simultaneous Peripheral Operations On-Line — disk-buffered queue for slow shared output devices (printers); allows multiple jobs to produce output without waiting for the physical device.

questions.question:
Which one of the following is true for a CPU having a single interrupt request line and a single interrupt grant line?
A. Neither vectored interrupt nor multiple interrupting devices are possible
B. Vectored interrupts are not possible but multiple interrupting devices are possible
C. Vectored interrupts are possible but multiple interrupting devices are not possible
D. Both vectored interrupts and multiple interrupting devices are possible
questions.answer: D. Both vectored interrupts and multiple interrupting devices are possible
questions.to_find: What is possible with a single interrupt request line and a single interrupt grant line

understand.plain: A CPU has a single INTR (interrupt request) line and a single INTA (interrupt acknowledge/grant) line. Multiple devices can be connected to the single INTR line using a daisy-chain or wired-OR arrangement. When the CPU grants an interrupt, devices can place a vector (address or interrupt number) on the data bus to identify themselves — this is vectored interrupt. So both vectored interrupts AND multiple devices are possible with just one INTR and one INTA line.

keywords.explain: An interrupt mechanism where the interrupting device supplies an interrupt vector (address/number) so the CPU jumps directly to the correct handler.
keywords.example: Device places vector 0x30 on data bus; CPU jumps to ISR at address stored at vector 0x30.

keywords.explain: A hardware scheme where multiple devices share one INTR line; the INTA signal is passed through devices in series; the first device that requested the interrupt intercepts INTA and places its vector on the bus.
keywords.example: Devices D1-D2-D3 are daisy-chained; D2 interrupted -> INTA travels D1->D2; D2 intercepts and puts vector on bus.

understand.visual_alt: Daisy-chain interrupt scheme: multiple devices share a wired-OR INTR line to CPU; INTA travels device to device; intercepting device puts its vector on data bus, enabling vectored interrupts.

given.aim: Determine what interrupt features are possible with one INTR line and one INTA line

terms.meaning: A single hardware wire connecting all devices to the CPU for signaling interrupts
terms.example: All devices wire-OR their interrupt signals onto this single line
terms.connects: Multiple devices can still share it via wired-OR or daisy-chain

terms.meaning: CPU's acknowledge signal sent back after accepting an interrupt request
terms.example: CPU lowers INTA to acknowledge; devices pass it along the daisy chain
terms.connects: Daisy-chain passes INTA through devices; the requesting device intercepts and supplies vector

terms.meaning: Mechanism where interrupting device places a unique vector on the data bus to identify its ISR
terms.example: Device puts 0x20 on data bus; CPU uses it to look up ISR address
terms.connects: Possible even with single INTR/INTA via daisy-chain; device intercepts INTA and puts vector on bus

given.plan: Analyze daisy-chain interrupt handling: multiple devices can share one INTR line (wired-OR); the INTA is passed device-to-device; whichever device requested the interrupt intercepts INTA and places its interrupt vector on the data bus. This supports both multiple devices AND vectored interrupts.

steps.title: Multiple devices on single INTR line
steps.apply: Multiple devices can be connected to a single INTR line using the wired-OR technique: any device asserting its interrupt signal drives the shared INTR line high. So multiple interrupting devices ARE possible with a single INTR line.
steps.note: This eliminates options A and C which claim multiple devices are not possible.

steps.title: Vectored interrupts with daisy-chain INTA
steps.apply: When the CPU asserts INTA (interrupt acknowledge) on the single INTA line, it travels through devices in a daisy-chain. The first device in the chain that requested an interrupt intercepts the INTA signal and places its interrupt vector on the data bus. The CPU reads this vector and jumps to the appropriate ISR. So vectored interrupts ARE possible with a single INTA line.
steps.note: This eliminates option B which claims vectored interrupts are not possible.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.apply: Both vectored interrupts and multiple interrupting devices are possible with a single INTR line (wired-OR for multiple devices) and a single INTA line (daisy-chain for vectored identification). Answer: D.
steps.note: This is a standard interrupt-driven I/O architecture used in many real systems.

solution.result: Both vectored interrupts and multiple interrupting devices are possible. Answer: D.

questions.formula_note: Daisy-chain interrupt acknowledgement: single INTR (wired-OR for multiple devices) and single INTA (passed serially through devices) enables both multiple devices and vectored interrupts.
